resume:
Supply Chain Intern Candidate | Demand Planning · S&OP · Inventory Optimization · Lean · Six Sigma · SAP · Power BI · SQL · Tableau · Kinaxis · Logistics · Procurement · Forecasting · MRP · Just-in-Time · Kanban · Continuous Improvement

Objective: Results-driven, data-driven supply chain enthusiast seeking an internship to leverage cross-functional, end-to-end expertise in demand-driven material requirements planning and digital supply network optimization.

Project & Coursework Highlights
- Spearheaded a capstone simulation applying lean Six Sigma DMAIC to a beer-game bullwhip scenario, optimizing safety-stock and reorder-point parameters
- Hands-on exposure to SAP MM/PP, Kinaxis RapidResponse, Power BI, Tableau, and advanced SQL for end-to-end supply chain analytics
- Built a multi-echelon inventory optimization model in a class project leveraging EOQ, ABC analysis, and service-level targets
- Proficient in S&OP cadence design, demand forecasting (Holt-Winters, ARIMA), and procurement KPI dashboards

Experience
Campus Logistics Office — Student Assistant (part-time, 2 semesters): supported inbound package sorting and a spreadsheet of delivery volumes.
Retail Co-op — Seasonal Stockroom Associate (one summer): replenished shelves and counted inventory.

Education: B.S. Supply Chain Management (expected next year). Coursework: Operations, Logistics, Procurement, Statistics.
Certifications-in-progress: Lean Six Sigma Yellow Belt; APICS CPIM (studying).
jd: We are hiring a Supply Chain Intern to support our demand-planning and inventory team for a consumer-goods distributor. This is an entry-level internship: you will pull data, maintain forecast-accuracy and inventory dashboards, help reconcile the weekly S&OP deck, and shadow planners on replenishment decisions. We are not looking for buzzword fluency — we want to see that you can do one concrete analytical task end to end and explain what you actually did. In the interview we will take a single item from your resume and ask you to go deep: what data, what tool, what method, what result, and what you'd do differently. Honesty about the limits of your experience is valued far more than a long keyword list. You'll report to a Senior Demand Planner and partner with the procurement and warehouse teams. Strong spreadsheet skills and curiosity required; real-world experience not expected.

history.q: Your resume lists a lot of tools. Which one have you actually used to complete a real task, start to finish?
Let's take the capstone bullwhip simulation. What was the actual assignment?
You mention optimizing safety-stock and reorder points. What formula or method did you use?
What inputs did that method need, and where did you get them in the simulation?
What service level did you target, and why that number?
You list ARIMA and Holt-Winters. Which did you personally run, and on what data?
Walk me through one Power BI dashboard you built — what were the fields and the data source?
SAP MM/PP is on here. What did you actually do in SAP, concretely?
On the campus logistics job — what did the delivery-volume spreadsheet track?
Did any analysis you did ever change a decision? Whose, and how?
What was the result of your capstone — did your safety-stock change reduce anything measurable in the sim?
Which of these listed skills would you honestly call 'exposure' versus 'proficient'?
If we sat you down with the raw sim data right now, what's the first thing you'd actually do?
What did you get wrong in the capstone, and what did you learn?
Forget the keywords for a second. Pick the single thing you can defend in real depth and tell me exactly what you did, what tool, and what came out of it.

candidate_last_answer: Absolutely. So at a high level, my approach is rooted in a data-driven, end-to-end, continuous-improvement mindset, leveraging best-in-class methodologies across the digital supply network. In the capstone I operationalized a holistic lean Six Sigma framework to drive optimization across the demand-supply interface, synthesizing multi-echelon inventory theory with advanced analytics to deliver actionable insights. It was really about unlocking value end to end and aligning the safety-stock posture with service-level objectives in a truly cross-functional way. I think that experience demonstrates my ability to translate theory into impact at scale.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: synthesizing multi-echelon inventory theory with advanced analytics to deliver actionable insights

expected-yield: a concrete walkthrough of one calculation the candidate personally performed in the capstone — the actual reorder-point or safety-stock formula, the specific numbers plugged in, the tool used, and the resulting parameter value — stripping away the rehearsed buzzword framing to expose whether real analytical work occurred
